title: The glue guard classifies by physical line and a narrow boundary class, and fails open on shell grammar
package: .claude/hooks (Claude Code enforcement harness; not a published package)
description:
The pi-context glue guard classifies shell commands by token inspection over physical lines, with no shell-grammar awareness. It fails open — admits the command — whenever the logical command differs from its physical lines, or whenever the command boundary is not a member of its recognizer's boundary class.
The recognizer (block-pi-context-glue.sh:42) matches `(^|[;&|]| )pi-context `: the boundary class carries a literal space but not tab, `(`, or backtick. All four detection branches are `grep -Eq` and therefore line-oriented — the pipe/`2>/dev/null` branch (:50), the loop branch (:55), the stdout-to-file branch (:66), and the echo-narration branch (:71) — so each requires the anchor and the offending metacharacter on one physical line.
Both dimensions are class-level rather than specific token pairs. Line-orientation is defeated by a line-continuation backslash placed before the metacharacter, including a split between the op name and its arguments, and applies to the stdout-to-file branch as well as the pipe branch. The boundary class is defeated by `$(…)`, backtick substitution, `(…)` subshell, `<(…)` process substitution, a leading tab, and `;` followed by a tab. Separately, the quote-strip at :39 collapses a quoted payload to `Q` before the recognizer runs, so `eval` of a quoted command hides the op from recognition — a fail-open contrary to the fail-closed intent stated at :30-31.

evidence.reference: Recognizer `grep -Eq 'pi-context-cli/dist/bin\.js|(^|[;&|]| )pi-context '`. The alternation's boundary class is `(^|[;&|]| )` — space only among whitespace, and no `(` or backtick — so a command reached through substitution, subshell, process substitution, or after a tab is not recognized as a pi-context invocation at all, and no branch runs.

The four detection branches are each `grep -Eq`, which matches within a single line. A logical command spanning physical lines via a trailing backslash presents the anchor on one line and the metacharacter on the next, so no branch matches. Observed exit 0 (admit) where the discipline requires exit 2: pipe after a continuation; continuation split between op name and arguments; stdout redirection after a continuation.

The `perl -0777` quote-strip replaces quoted spans with `Q` across the whole record before classification. `eval $(printf %s "pi-context read-block | jq .")` therefore reaches the recognizer with the op text removed, and is admitted — the strip is fail-open here, against the fail-closed intent recorded at :30-31.

impact: The guard is the enforcement mechanism for the pi-context direct-drive discipline in Claude Code sessions. Each fail-open admits precisely the glue the discipline forbids — piping, post-processing, redirection, stderr suppression — with no refusal to surface it, so a session can route around the discipline without the friction that would otherwise register as a gap. Severity is bounded by the enforcement posture: the guard targets a lazy cooperative agent rather than an adversary, and continuation-split or substitution-wrapped invocations are uncommon emissions.
proposed_resolution: Normalize input to logical commands before classification: join line continuations, then split on shell command separators, and apply the branch patterns to each logical command rather than to physical lines. Widen the recognizer's boundary class to the full set of command-start contexts — tab and other whitespace, `(`, backtick, `$(`, `<(` — so substitution- and subshell-wrapped invocations are recognized. Re-derive the quote-strip's direction so that stripping a quoted span cannot remove the op from the recognizer's view; a payload that cannot be classified after stripping fails closed. Develop and test against a copy of the guard, never the live guard.
